title: How I Got the Canada Vanier CGS Scholarship
summary: Apply early and prepare transcripts. The Vanier CGS requires a 3.7+ GPA and strong research proposal.
story:
I started preparing 8 months before the deadline. The Vanier Canada Graduate Scholarship (CGS) is one of the most prestigious awards for doctoral students. I remember the exact moment I decided to apply — sitting in a cybercafe in Yaba, Lagos, with slow internet and high dreams.

The first step was reaching out to a potential supervisor at the University of British Columbia. I sent over 20 emails before getting one response. That one response changed everything. My supervisor was crucial to strengthening my research proposal.

For the application itself, I needed three reference letters, an academic CV, and a detailed research proposal. I rewrote my proposal seven times. Each revision made it sharper. I also needed official transcripts from every institution I had attended — getting those from my Nigerian university took two months alone.

My advice: start contacting supervisors at least 10 months before the deadline. The Canadian embassy in Lagos can verify documents, which adds credibility. Budget about 150,000 naira for the entire process including transcript notarization, English proficiency tests, and application fees.
tips: Start early. Get strong recommendation letters from professors who know your research. Proofread your essays multiple times. Join online communities of Nigerian scholars abroad — they have insider tips.
author: Anonymous Student

title: FUPRE Admission Process 2024 — What They Don't Tell You
summary: Upload documents before deadline. JAMB score of 220+ recommended. The portal crashes near deadlines.
story:
Getting into the Federal University of Petroleum Resources Effurun (FUPRE) was a journey I was not prepared for. I had a JAMB score of 234 and Post-UTME score of 71%, which should have been straightforward. It was not.

The FUPRE admission portal is notorious. I learned this the hard way when I waited until 3 days before the document upload deadline and found the portal completely unresponsive. I spent two days in a business center trying repeatedly. Eventually, I got through at 2am on the final day.

Document requirements included: O'level results, JAMB result slip, birth certificate (not baptismal card), letter of origin, and passport photographs with white background. My first submission was rejected because my passport photo background was slightly off-white.

Accommodation near FUPRE is genuinely difficult. The main hostels fill up in the first week. I recommend contacting the Students' Union at FUPRE before resumption to get accommodation contacts.
tips: Have all documents scanned and ready before the portal opens. Don't wait until the last day. Join the unofficial FUPRE WhatsApp groups — they share real-time updates when deadlines change.
author: Tunde A.

title: UK Student Visa Interview — What to Expect
summary: Be confident, know your course details, and explain your funding clearly. The officer will probe your ties to Nigeria.
story:
My UK student visa interview at the British High Commission in Abuja was scheduled for 8am. I arrived at 7:15am and there were already about 40 people in the queue. The process is more organized than I expected.

After biometrics and document checks, I waited about 2 hours before my interview. The officer was professional but probing. She asked: Why this university specifically? Why this course? How are you funding it? What do you plan to do after graduation? Do you have family in the UK?

The ties-to-Nigeria question was the most important one. I explained that I was returning to work at my father's engineering firm, and I had a letter from the firm to prove it. This seemed to satisfy her.

My visa was approved in 15 working days. The entire UK application process including visa cost me approximately 850,000 naira.
tips: Practice common questions out loud before the interview. Bring ALL supporting documents even if not required — bank statements, acceptance letter, accommodation booking. The officer can sense nervousness.
author: Chioma O.

title: Finding Safe Affordable Housing Near UNILAG
summary: Found safe accommodation for 80k per session near UNILAG main gate. Key is starting the search 6 weeks before resumption.
story:
After spending my 100-level year in the official UNILAG hostel (which had its own challenges), I decided to look for off-campus accommodation. The search took me nearly 4 weeks.

The areas around UNILAG — Akoka, Bariga, and Abule-Oja — have a huge range of options. I found that the price jumps significantly as you get closer to the main gate. The sweet spot is about a 10-minute walk away, where prices are reasonable but the area is still relatively safe.

I paid 80,000 naira for a self-contained room for the academic session (about 10 months). This is on the lower end — some rooms in the same area go for 150,000 naira. The difference is usually AC versus fan, and quality of plumbing.

My landlord required 6 months upfront which was difficult. I eventually found one who accepted 3 months upfront with a guarantor form.
tips: Start searching 6 weeks before school resumes, not 2 weeks. Bring a trusted adult or older student when viewing accommodation. Never pay without a receipt and a signed agreement.
author: Anonymous

title: Landing a Remote Internship as a Nigerian Student
summary: Landed a remote marketing internship through consistent Twitter (X) networking. No connection needed, just value.
story:
In my second year at school, I started posting marketing analyses of Nigerian brands on Twitter. Nothing fancy — just breakdowns of campaigns that caught my attention and what I thought worked or didn't work.

After about 3 months of consistent posting, I got a DM from the founder of a Lagos-based fintech startup. She had been following my posts and was impressed by my thinking. She offered me a remote marketing internship at 40,000 naira per month.

The work was real: I managed their social media, wrote email newsletters, and helped with launch campaigns for two new products. I learned more in 4 months than in a full semester of marketing classes.

The key was consistency and genuine analysis — not just posting for the sake of posting.
tips: Pick one platform and post consistently about your field. Engage with practitioners, not just other students. Show your thinking, not just your credentials.
author: Ibrahim K.

title: Commonwealth Scholarship Application — Full Walkthrough
summary: The Commonwealth Scholarship is fully funded and highly competitive. Your personal statement is everything.
story:
The Commonwealth Scholarship Commission offers fully funded masters scholarships for Nigerian students — tuition, flights, living allowance, and a warm-clothing allowance. It sounds too good to be true, but it is real and Nigerians have won it.

I applied during my final year of undergraduate study. The process starts with getting a conditional offer from a UK university first — the scholarship application requires this. I applied to 5 universities, got offers from 3, and chose my preferred one for the scholarship application.

The personal statement is the most critical element. The CSC wants to know your development impact plan: how will your postgraduate study improve lives back in Nigeria? I spent 3 weeks on my 500-word personal statement, getting feedback from two professors and a senior civil servant.

Final result: I was selected as a Scholar. It changed my trajectory.
tips: Your development impact plan must be specific and credible. Vague statements about 'contributing to Nigeria's development' will not work. Name specific communities, sectors, and interventions.
author: Adaeze M.

title: OAU Postgraduate Admission — My Experience
summary: Obafemi Awolowo University's postgraduate admission is competitive but well-structured. The entrance exam matters.
story:
Getting into OAU for postgraduate studies was very different from undergraduate. The process is more structured and the competition is real — I was applying for Economics and there were over 200 candidates for 30 spots.

The OAU postgraduate entrance exam covers your specific discipline plus a general section on critical reasoning. I prepared for 6 weeks using past questions which I found in a Facebook group for OAU PG applicants. The pass mark varies by department.

After the exam, there is an oral interview with the department faculty. This is where subject knowledge really matters. They asked me to defend my undergraduate dissertation and explain current economic trends affecting Nigeria.

One thing that surprised me: the departmental fees and levies are significant on top of the official school fees. Budget an extra 60,000-100,000 naira for this.
tips: Join OAU-specific communities early — Facebook groups and WhatsApp groups have past questions and real insider information. Budget for unofficial departmental fees from day one.
author: Kayode S.

title: US F-1 Student Visa — Passed on First Try
summary: The US F-1 visa interview in Lagos is intense. Preparation, confidence, and strong ties to Nigeria are key.
story:
The US Embassy in Lagos has a reputation for being difficult, and the F-1 (student) visa is particularly challenging for Nigerian applicants. I was applying to a Masters program in Computer Science at Georgia Tech — a legitimate program with full funding. I was still nervous.

I prepared by joining an online community of Nigerians who had been through the process. They shared actual questions asked and what worked. The most important advice: be brief and direct. The officer has limited time. Long explanations read as nervousness or deception.

My interview lasted exactly 3 minutes and 20 seconds. The questions: What program? Which university? Who is funding you? Do you have family in the US? What will you do after graduation? I answered each in 2-3 sentences maximum.

Approved. The DS-160, SEVIS fee, and visa fee cost me about 250 USD total.
tips: Practice answering in SHORT sentences — 2-3 per question maximum. Know your I-20 inside and out. Have your funding documentation on top of your pile. Strong ties to Nigeria (job offer, family business, property) matter enormously.
author: Emeka J.
